# Integrating Decentralized Identity (DID) and Vendor‑Neutral IAM in Capability‑Native Agent Governance

## Executive Summary: A Hybrid Identity & Capability Approach

**Recommendation:** Adopt a **hybrid identity architecture** that combines decentralized identity (DID) and verifiable credentials (VCs) with your capability-based authorization framework. This will allow autonomous AI agents to prove **who they are** and **who they represent** in a portable, cross-domain manner, while still leveraging enterprise identity systems for initial trust establishment. In practice, this means:

*   **Give each agent a Decentralized Identifier (DID)** (e.g. a DID anchored in DNS via `did:web`, or on a public ledger via `did:ion` or other methods) and associated cryptographic keys. Agents use these keys to sign capability tokens or present verifiable credentials, so that any service can authenticate the agent *without calling back to a central IdP* each time. This eliminates vendor lock-in at runtime and enables multi-cloud/multi-org operability. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Issue agent capabilities as Verifiable Credentials (VCs)** or similarly signed tokens that embed fine-grained permissions and delegation chains. For example, when a human or service grants an agent permission, it issues a VC (signed by the issuer’s DID key) listing the specific **actions and constraints** allowed. These credentials can be verified by any party that trusts the issuer’s DID (or trust registry), making the system **vendor-agnostic** and decentralized in verification. Crucially, these capability credentials have their own lifecycle: they can expire or be revoked independently of the agent’s core identity. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Maintain a unified enforcement layer (policy engine)** that is identity-neutral. At runtime, services and tools enforce access by validating the presented capability tokens (signatures, scopes, expiry) and do **not** require live queries to an identity provider. This preserves the **“no runtime IAM dependency”** principle of capability-based security – even as identity issuance becomes more distributed. Essentially, identity is used at issuance and delegation time; once a capability is issued, the data-plane checks are purely cryptographic and local.

This hybrid model offers the best of both worlds:

*   *Enterprise control and accountability:* The enterprise (or another trusted authority) is involved in issuing or approving the initial capabilities, and human owners are traceable via the DID chain. Every agent action can be traced back, via cryptographic delegation chains, to a human or organizational DID as the root of trust. This satisfies accountability requirements by ensuring *“every agent action must trace to a human or authorised organisational principal”*. [\[ietf.org\]](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-singla-agent-identity-protocol-01.html)
*   *Decentralized verification and multi-org trust:* Relying parties (APIs, tools, partner systems) can authenticate and authorize an agent’s requests by verifying the agent’s DID signature and attached credentials, **without relying on a shared vendor-specific IdP**. The DID and VC standards provide a neutral, open infrastructure – *“analogous to HTTP, OAuth, and JWT”* – that avoids proprietary dependencies. This is crucial for agents operating across cloud providers or companies that lack a common SSO domain. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Granular delegation with least privilege:* Capabilities (as VCs or tokens) precisely encode what an agent can do – e.g., *“read DB.table1 but not write”* or *“cancel booking #12345 before date X”*. These can be **attenuated and re-issued** down a chain of sub-agents, with cryptographic proof of each delegation. A parent agent can only delegate a subset of its own rights (no ambient authority leakage), enforced by design (e.g., the IETF Agent Identity Protocol mandates *“a child agent must not be granted capabilities the parent doesn’t hold”*, fulfilling the no-new-privileges rule). This ensures cross-organizational delegation is safe and limited to intended scope.

**Trade-off:** This approach introduces complexity in key management, DID resolution, and credential revocation compared to a single-IDP solution. A fully decentralized system must handle public key discovery (resolving DIDs to obtain agent and issuer public keys), **revocation checking** (e.g., maintaining a global status list or registry), and **performance tuning** (to avoid slowing down agent interactions with excessive cryptographic checks). We address these challenges with a combination of design strategies:

*   Using **fit-for-purpose DID methods** (e.g., `did:web` anchored in DNS for low-latency resolution within enterprise contexts, and `did:ion` or other ledger-based DIDs for broader ecosystem identifiers) depending on use case.
*   Imposing **short token lifetimes** (minutes) so that compromised credentials naturally expire quickly, and using overlay networks or push-notification systems for near-real-time revocations when needed.
*   Tiering security controls by risk: e.g., lightweight in-domain interactions can rely on offline CRL checks or did:key identifiers for speed, whereas high-risk cross-domain transactions demand live status verification and proof-of-possession of keys (such as OAuth DPoP or mTLS) to prevent token theft.
*   Providing robust automation for key and credential management (e.g., using **SPIFFE** within a trust domain to issue short-lived X.509 or JWT credentials to agent processes, which can then be bound to DIDs for external use). [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/), [\[spiffe.io\]](https://spiffe.io/docs/latest/spiffe-about/overview/?ref=blog.identity.foundation)

Overall, integrating DIDs and VCs into the capability-based agent governance strengthens **federation and zero-trust** properties. Agents get portable, verifiable identities, and authorizations become self-contained and transparently transferable across system boundaries. The result is a future-proof foundation for multi-cloud and multi-organization agent ecosystems, addressing the shortcomings of both traditional identity systems and standalone capability tokens.

The sections below provide a detailed analysis of the alternatives and a recommended architecture, followed by a comparison of when to favor each approach.

***

## Rethinking Identity & Authorization for Autonomous Agents

Modern autonomous agents break the assumptions of traditional identity and access management systems. Table 1 highlights how a distributed identity/capability approach contrasts with conventional IAM and why a hybrid model is needed:

**Table 1. Traditional Identity vs. Decentralized Identity vs. Hybrid Approach**

| Aspect                       | **Centralized IAM (OAuth/OIDC, SAML)**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | **Decentralized Identity (DIDs + VCs)**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | **Hybrid Model (Recommended)**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Trust Scope**              | Single domain or federated circle. Relies on known identity providers (e.g., corporate IdP or OIDC provider). Cross-org trust requires pre-established federation or exchange of API keys [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/).                                                                                                                                                  | Global namespace of trust via cryptographic DIDs. Any party can verify identity or credentials using public ledgers or decentralized PKI, no prior federation needed [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/).                                                                                                                                                                                  | Enterprise-issued identities augmented by DIDs for portability. Internal interactions use standard IAM; external interactions use DIDs/VCs for trust without bespoke federation [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| **Agent Identity**           | Typically borrowed from a human user or a static service identity (impersonation) [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). Multiple agents might share one identity (no differentiation) [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/).                                                                                             | Each agent has a unique self-controlled identity (DID) with its own keys and metadata. Not tied to any single vendor or human account [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). Can include info on agent’s origin or type in DID Document.                                                                                                                                                     | Each agent gets a stable DID-based identity (could be derived from enterprise identity or created on the fly). The enterprise can also maintain a mapping to human owners or internal IDs for accountability.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| **Authentication**           | Token asserts *who the user is*, issued by IdP. Relying party must validate token by trusting the IdP’s keys or calling back to it. Typically one token = one principal with broad rights [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/).                                                                                                                                                  | Agent proves control of its DID (e.g., by signing a challenge or using DID-based authentication protocols). No central authority needed per transaction; trust is in the cryptographic signature and DID registry.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | Agents authenticate with their own keys (DID auth) against internal systems too. The enterprise IdP may itself use DIDs (e.g., issue a VC that binds a DID to a user or policy), reducing direct coupling to the IdP at runtime.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **Authorization Model**      | Often coarse: user or client app gets an OAuth token covering broad scopes for a service (impersonation of user). Delegation beyond one level is unsupported [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). Hard to represent “user A allowed agent B to do X on C’s behalf”.                                                                                                             | **Object-capability model**: explicit tokens/VCs grant specific actions on specific resources. Supports attenuation (narrowing) and delegation by cryptographic chaining of credentials [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). Each delegated credential links back to the original grantor (e.g., human principal’s DID) via signatures and chain identifiers.                              | Enterprise issues the initial capability to the agent (acting as the first issuer in the chain). The agent may further delegate with sub-credentials as needed. Within a trust domain, a simpler form (JWT-based macaroons or SPIFFE SVIDs) may be used; when crossing to another domain, the agent presents a DID+VC that the external party can verify.                                                                                                                                                                    |
| **Delegation & Attenuation** | Largely unsupported or done via impersonation. OAuth/OIDC tokens cannot natively represent a chain of delegated rights [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). Workarounds (sharing tokens or using one service as a proxy for another) lead to **confused deputy** vulnerabilities [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). | Built-in support for delegation chains: an agent’s credential can embed or reference the credential of the entity that empowered it (principal’s DID, parent agent’s credential, etc.). Attenuation (subsetting permissions) is achieved by issuing new credentials with reduced scopes/constraints.                                                                                                                                                 | The capability issuer encodes *who delegated what to whom* in each credential. Internally, a simple chain (user → agent) might suffice; for multi-hop delegation or trust boundary crossing, incorporate the full DID-based delegation chain (e.g., human DID → enterprise agent VC → partner agent VC). Enforcement logic ensures no agent can exceed the delegating principal’s rights (no-new-privilege invariants).                                                                                                      |
| **Revocation & Expiry**      | Typically medium-to-long token lifespans (hours to days) for usability. Revocation is limited: either short lifetimes or active invalidation via token introspection lists, which require the resource server to contact the issuer. Cross-IdP revocation is cumbersome or impossible.                                                                                                                                                    | Flexible but potentially complex. Capabilities/VCs can be issued with **short TTLs** for automatic expiry, and issuers can publish **revocation registries or status lists** on DLTs or via web services [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). Verifiers must check revocation status (like CRLs/OCSP in PKI) – which can be optimized by *push-based updates* or tiered trust (see below). | Use layered revocation: for internal services, a centralized kill-switch or status service can push revocation events (low latency). For external verification, include revocation info in a **transparency log or registry** accessible via the DID Document (e.g., an `AIPRegistry` service endpoint in the principal’s DID Document lists revoked agent credentials, similar to certificate transparency). Additionally, keep default expirations short (minutes) for all capability tokens to limit the window of abuse. |
| **Auditability**             | Actions typically recorded as “User X (or Client Y) did Z”. Hard to untangle if X’s agent actually performed the action, or which of multiple agents was responsible [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/). No cryptographic proof tying the action back to a specific agent instance.                                                                                            | Every capability token is signed by a specific issuer (human or agent DID), yielding a provable chain of custody for permissions. Logs can store these token identifiers or entire credentials, so investigators can reconstruct *who authorized each action and when* with high confidence. Non-repudiation is enhanced by crypto signatures at each delegation hop.                                                                                | The system logs the **principal chain and capability ID** for each action. For internal events, an audit service can resolve human-friendly details (e.g., which user’s agent). For cross-org interactions, the logs plus the DID/VCs allow independent third-party verification of the delegation chain if needed (useful for compliance and forensics) [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/).                                                                      |

**Key takeaway:** **Identity-based authentication alone is insufficient in an agentic, multi-organizational environment; capability-based authorization alone is powerful but lacks transparency of *who* ultimately is responsible.** The emerging consensus (from standards bodies and industry alike) is that **both are needed, structured as separate layers**: one layer to establish *who* the agent is and on whose behalf it operates (via DIDs and a delegated chain of credentials), and another to define *what actions* it’s allowed to take (via capability tokens/VCs). In this layered model, identity proves accountability and enables audit across domains, while capabilities enforce actual permissions and safety at a granular level. The combination is reflected in the proposed Agent Identity Protocol (AIP) standard, which defines: [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

[\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

***

## Comparative Analysis: Centralized vs Decentralized vs Hybrid IAM

**Centralized Identity (Traditional IAM, OAuth/OIDC/SAML):** In a single-cloud or single-organization setting, it’s natural to use an existing identity provider (e.g., Azure AD/Entra ID, AWS IAM, or an internal PKI) to authenticate users and even to authenticate agents. This was the starting point for our initial design: e.g., using the user’s Azure AD identity and role to decide what capabilities to give an agent. Within one security domain, this is straightforward, and tools can verify tokens by checking signatures against the known IdP’s keys or via direct introspection. However, this model **struggles in agentic scenarios**:

*   *Impersonation vs. delegation:* OAuth/OIDC tokens typically indicate **full user impersonation** rather than constrained delegation. If an agent uses a user’s access token, services see the user as the actor and may assume all actions were directly user-initiated, obscuring the agent’s involvement. This undermines accountability – you lose clarity on which agent did what on whose behalf. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *No support for sub-delegation:* Traditional tokens cannot represent a chain of delegations (user → service → sub-agent). The token’s authority is monolithic, tied to an identity or a static role. Agents end up either with *no access or full access*, since intermediate layers can’t down-scope on the fly. This is one reason compromised agents are so dangerous: an OAuth token typically lets them act as a fully-empowered user or service account. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Federation and vendor lock-in:* Crossing organizational or cloud-provider boundaries with OAuth or SAML requires bilateral trust agreements (federation) or sharing API keys, which doesn’t scale to open ecosystems. Each integration is custom, and a malicious or compromised identity provider can undermine the whole arrangement. As the Cloud Security Alliance notes, **traditional IdM protocols can’t keep up with the autonomy and ephemerality of AI agents**. [\[cloudsecur...liance.org\]](https://cloudsecurityalliance.org/artifacts/agentic-ai-identity-and-access-management-a-new-approach)
*   *Lack of context in tokens:* Identity tokens usually carry coarse scopes or roles. They don’t encode dynamic context like “this agent is allowed to perform action X *for the next 5 minutes* on resource Y because user Z approved it.” Trying to include such context in natural language (prompt-based policies) is brittle and non-verifiable. Traditional IAM’s coarse granularity forces either over-provisioning or constant human involvement, both unacceptable at scale. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Difficult revocation and monitoring:* Centralized tokens are often long-lived to reduce login friction, and revoking them is all-or-nothing (you revoke the user’s entire session or the application’s entire access). If one agent misbehaves, you might have to kill the credentials that affect many agents or user sessions, as they’re shared. Moreover, logs will show only that “User X’s token did something”, providing no built-in way to pinpoint *which agent* among possibly hundreds was responsible. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

**Decentralized Identity & Verifiable Credentials:** A DID-based approach addresses these gaps by giving every agent a unique, verifiable identity and using **credentials as the vehicle for authorization**. In effect, it externalizes what was “ambient” in the centralized model:

*   *Every agent is a first-class identity principal:* Instead of multiple agents sharing one identity, each agent (even ephemeral ones) can have a cryptographically verifiable DID. For instance, an agent could be identified by a DID of the form `did:ion:EiBc...` (if using the public Ion network on Bitcoin) or `did:web:client.example.com:agent:123` (if using a DNS-based DID). The DID Document would contain the agent’s public keys and possibly metadata like an endpoint for its **Capability Manifest** or status (à la AIP’s serviceEndpoint for an AIP registry). This DID can be used across systems – any service with a DID resolver can fetch the keys and verify signatures, achieving **SSO without a central SSO server**. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Verifiable, least-privilege credentials:* Instead of one big token that implicitly conveys broad authority, the agent carries explicit credentials for each permission. The W3C **Verifiable Credential (VC)** format (JSON-LD or JWT) supports encoding arbitrary claims with issuer signatures. A profile of VC known as **ZCAP-LD (Capability-based Linked Data Authorization)** is specifically designed to represent *delegable, attenuable permissions* in a verifiable way. For example, a VC might say *“Issuer DID X attests that agent DID Y may perform action Z on resource W under conditions Q until time T”* (with all those elements cryptographically signed by X). If agent Y later spawns agent Z, Y can act as an issuer and sign a narrower VC for Z (attesting “agent Z can perform a subset of my actions”) – this is a formal delegation. The chain of DID signatures (X → Y → Z) is attached to Z’s credential, so when Z tries to act, a relying service can verify the entire chain of trust and ensure it roots back in a known authority. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Cross-domain trust via DIDs:* The heavy lifting of cross-organizational trust is shifted to **DID infrastructure**. Parties agree on which DID *methods* or *specific issuer DIDs* they trust, analogous to how web browsers trust a set of root certificate authorities. For example, a bank’s API might be configured to trust any credential issued by a DID under `did:web:yourcompany.com` (meaning your company’s DNS domain vouches for the agent) or by any DID signed by a particular consortium ledger. Because DIDs are **globally resolvable and controlled by the issuing entity** (not by a central provider at use time), this model scales to many participants without one-to-one integrations. In essence, the trust model becomes *“bring your own identity”*: as long as your agent presents a credential from a trusted issuer (which could be your enterprise, a partner, or even the end-user themselves via self-sovereign attestations), it gains the specified access. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Detached, cryptographic audit trails:* Every capability credential can be logged and later independently verified. There’s no need to rely solely on a single server’s log entry saying “X did Y”; you can examine the chain of signed credentials to see exactly who authorized X to do Y, and validate those signatures against DIDs (even offline). This fulfills non-repudiation and forensic needs. As the IETF’s AIP draft notes, *“every agent action is cryptographically attributable to the human or organisational principal that authorised it”*. This is a significant compliance advantage when facing regulations that demand **traceability of AI decisions** or when investigating incidents across company lines. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

**Challenges of a fully decentralized approach:** The most cited downsides are complexity and latency:

*   Setting up a **DID infrastructure** (especially ledger-based methods) can introduce operational overhead. Resolving a DID like `did:ion` might involve queries to a blockchain or decentralized network, which are slower than a local database query. However, methods like `did:web` piggyback on DNS/HTTPS for resolution for faster performance (with the trade-off of trusting DNS/PKI rather than a blockchain). [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Revocation and real-time risk mitigation** are harder without a central authority. In a pure peer-to-peer model, how does one agent or service learn that a particular credential or key should no longer be honored (e.g., agent compromised)? Solutions include:
    *   *Short-lived credentials:* Issue capabilities with very short expiration (e.g., 5 minutes) so that revocation becomes as simple as not renewing the credential. This is how AIP handles baseline revocation: *Tier 2 tokens (default for sensitive ops) are limited to 5-minute lifetime, so worst-case an attacker’s window is very small*.
    *   *Revocation registries / status lists:* For longer-lived credentials, maintain an append-only log (on-chain or off-chain) of revoked credentials or DIDs. The **AIP model introduces a Registry** that publishes a CRL (certificate revocation list) with a promise of a 15-minute update SLA, and also supports a real-time push notification protocol (RPNP) to get revocation events to verifiers within \~5 seconds. Agents’ DID Documents list their “AIP Registry” service endpoint for lookups – conceptually similar to OCSP responders in PKI.
    *   *Kill switches with DIDs:* If an enterprise needs to revoke all credentials of a compromised agent immediately, it can issue a global “revocation credential” or update a revocation field in the agent’s DID Document (if the DID method supports it). In practice, a central coordination service is still useful: for example, your company could operate a **trust registry** service where partner systems can quickly check the status of agent DIDs or capability IDs. This doesn’t have to be a single point of failure; it can be distributed or mirrored (and in AIP, the registry’s address itself is discovered via the issuer’s DID Document).
    *   *Multiple security tiers:* Recognizing the performance trade-offs, the AIP specification defines three “Grant Tiers” with escalating security requirements. Low-risk interactions (Tier 1) might allow offline revocation checking (cached CRLs) and even use a `did:key` (peer-generated key DID) for speed. Medium-risk (Tier 2) requires online revocation checks and stronger DID verification (principals **must use** globally resolvable DIDs like `did:web` or `did:ion` for these). High-risk (Tier 3) might require human pre-approvals and multi-sig or hardware-bound keys. The idea is to balance security and performance by tailoring the mechanism to the risk of the action.

*   **Adoption and interoperability:** DIDs and VCs are emerging technologies (W3C standards as of 2020–2023) and are seeing growing adoption (e.g., for user credentials in some government and financial contexts). For autonomous agents, these standards are very new – efforts like the Decentralized Identity Foundation’s Trusted AI Agents group and the IETF draft we cited show active development as of 2025–2026. Tooling and libraries exist (e.g., DID resolvers, credential libraries), but engineers will face a learning curve in implementing and operating these systems. By contrast, traditional OAuth/OIDC has mature libraries and known patterns. A hybrid approach lets you leverage existing IAM for as long as needed (for human sign-on and internal API auth) while incrementally introducing DIDs/VCs for inter-agent and cross-org authentication. This is in line with industry guidance: *“start exposing external agents with decentralised identifiers. Orgs can maintain existing OAuth for internal systems while giving agents portable identities for cross-boundary interactions”*. In other words, you don’t have to scrap your IdM investment – you augment it for the new use cases. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

## Architectural Implications and Design for Hybrid DID + Capability System

To incorporate DIDs and vendor-neutral auth into the capability architecture, we propose the following enhancements:

**1. Agent Identity with DIDs:** Each autonomous agent is assigned a DID at creation:

*   *Method choice:* Use **`did:web`** for agents that will primarily interact with parties that trust your DNS domain or certificate authority. For example, `did:web:enterprise.com:agents:agent123` can be resolved via a well-known URL on `enterprise.com` hosting the agent’s DID Document. This piggybacks on the existing DNS/HTTPS trust (your domain’s certificate) – convenient for partners who already trust your web PKI. Alternatively, use **`did:ion`** (or other **ledger-based DIDs**) for a more decentralized trust anchor. Did:ion (based on Sidetree on Bitcoin) provides globally verifiable DIDs without a central authority, at the cost of higher latency for updates (on the order of minutes to anchor new operations). For completely internal agents, you might also leverage **`did:peer`** or **`did:key`** (which require no external registry at all) for lightweight identity, though these are not easily verifiable by third parties and lack built-in revocation mechanisms. In practice, an enterprise might use did:web for any agent that may call external APIs (so partners can resolve it easily) and did:peer for purely internal ephemeral agents. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *DID Document:* The agent’s DID Document will list one or more public keys (for signature verification), and possibly service endpoints. We recommend including a service endpoint for a **Capability Registry** or status URL (if using AIP-style architecture) so verifiers know where to check for the agent’s current permission manifest or revocation status. For example, the DID Document for a human principal might contain:
    ```json
    {
      "id": "did:web:alice.example.com",
      "service": [{
         "id": "#aip-registry",
         "type": "AIPRegistry",
         "serviceEndpoint": "https://registry.example.com/agents/alicedelegations"
      }]
    }
    ```
    This indicates that any agent operating under Alice’s authority will have its delegation and capability manifests published at her registry service. An enterprise or consortium could run a **decentralized registry** (e.g., a transparency ledger or a cloud service) that aggregates and serves the latest known valid capabilities and revocations for agents under that principal. This is one way to scale revocation checking without a single point of failure.

**2. Verifiable Credential Issuance (Vendor-Neutral Authorization):** When granting capabilities to an agent, the capability issuer (which might be an enterprise IAM component or a user’s agent wallet) will create a **VC or signed token** that encapsulates the permission. Key considerations:

*   *Standard schemas:* To maximize interoperability, follow emerging standards for expressing capabilities in credentials. One option is **ZCAP-LD (OAuth-like Zcap-Linked Data)**, a W3C Community report which encodes capabilities in a JSON-LD document with an associated proof, supporting *“proof of possession”* and attenuation by reference. Another is the format defined in the AIP draft: it uses JWT-formatted “**Capability Manifests**” and “**Credential Tokens**” carrying an `aip_scope` (the actions/resources allowed) and an `aip_chain` for delegation info. Both approaches aim to be deterministic and easily verifiable by any service with the issuer’s public key. Ideally, these credentials become an **open standard** adopted across industry – AIP’s authors explicitly state their goal of a neutral, vendor-independent spec registered with W3C and IETF. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
*   *Principal delegation chain:* The credential must link back to the ultimate authority. In AIP, every **Credential Token** presented by an agent includes an array `aip_chain` where index 0 is the human/organization’s credential, and subsequent entries are intermediate delegations. The root is required to be a DID that is **not** the special `did:aip` (agent method), e.g. a human’s DID could be `did:web:enterprise.com:employees:alice` or a user’s self-sovereign `did:ion:...`. This ensures the chain begins with a real-world identity outside the agent network. The chain elements are usually structured as JWTs or VCs as well (in AIP, the “Principal Token” is a JWT containing the human’s DID and any human authentication proof, like OIDC token claims, signed by the enterprise IdP or another identity authority). The key point is that *the chain provides a cryptographic answer to “who ultimately authorized this agent?”* spanning multiple delegation steps.
*   *Embedding vs. referencing manifests:* There are two patterns to include capabilities in the token presented to a service:
    1.  **Fat token** – Include all details of allowed actions directly in the credential. For instance, a VC might list numerous permissions (like an OAuth JWT with multiple scopes). This makes verification one-step (just verify the VC’s signature and parse it). But tokens can become large, and if an agent has many permissions or constraints (e.g., a complex JSON policy), the token might be unwieldy to pass around.
    2.  **Thin token + registry lookup** – The token contains a reference (like a manifest ID or URL) to a capability set stored in a registry or database. In AIP’s design, the **Capability Manifest** is a JSON document stored in a Registry, and the agent’s Credential Token contains just a reference to it (by hash/ID) plus the delegation chain. The service then queries the registry (whose location it discovers via the issuer’s DID Document) to retrieve the full list of capabilities and constraints. This keeps the token size small and allows dynamic updates (e.g., the issuer could update the manifest to tighten or revoke permissions, and the service will fetch the latest version). The trade-off is the service must perform an extra lookup to the registry, which may impact latency or require the registry to be highly available. A hybrid approach could allow critical, time-sensitive permissions to be in the token itself, and further details or less-used constraints in an external manifest.
*   *Format neutrality:* Ensure the system can accept both **JWT-based tokens (e.g., with JWS signatures)** and **LD-Proof-based VCs**. Some ecosystems (enterprise and cloud) have better support for JWT (which can be processed with standard JOSE libraries and often piggy-back on OAuth tooling) while others might use JSON-LD and LD-Proofs for alignment with W3C standards. A truly vendor-agnostic system should not be tied to one format. For instance, AIP uses JWTs with a custom `AIP+JWT` type and Ed25519 or ES256 signatures, whereas ZCAP-LD uses LD-Proofs with keys from the DID Document. Both achieve similar ends. We might implement support for one primary format in the pilot and ensure an upgrade path for the other.

**3. Binding Enterprise IAM to DIDs and VCs:** In a hybrid model, your existing IAM (e.g., Azure AD) doesn’t disappear – it becomes one **issuer of identity or attributes** in the DID/VC ecosystem:

*   *Enterprise DID as root of trust:* The organization should itself have a DID (or multiple DIDs for different departments or services). For example, your company could use a `did:web:yourcompany.com` or a `did:ion` that it controls. This DID and its public keys will be the anchor for many credentials – effectively playing a similar role to an OAuth Authorization Server, but in a decentralized way. Partners and services must trust credentials signed by this DID to give your agents access. (In practice, trust may be established via an allowlist of known issuer DIDs, or via an out-of-band exchange of DID Documents, similar to exchanging SAML metadata.)
*   *Mapping internal identities to DIDs:* Users and services in your organization can also be given DIDs, or you can use a protocol like **Self-Issued OpenID Provider (SIOP) with OIDC for Verifiable Presentations** to bridge existing OAuth tokens into VCs. For example, when a user authorizes an agent, the system could issue a verifiable credential that contains the user’s enterprise ID (or a pseudonymous identifier) along with the approved agent permissions, signed by the enterprise DID. This essentially wraps the output of your existing IAM (the user’s authenticated identity and their policy decision) into a portable form. The IETF AIP draft calls this a **“Principal Token”** – essentially a JWT that contains the user’s DID (the subject) and is signed by the enterprise IdP (which itself might be identified by a DID). The principal’s DID could simply be a `did:web` pointing to a JSON document on your enterprise site that contains the user’s public key or a reference to the enterprise’s master DID plus the user’s ID.
*   *Onboarding external agents or users:* In a multi-org scenario, an agent from a partner company would present a VC from its own organization’s DID or a mutually trusted third party. For instance, if a partner’s agent needs to access your API, it might present a VC issued by *their* company’s DID that attests “Agent X is acting on behalf of Company Y with role Z”. Your system would verify that DID against a list of partner DIDs that are allowed for certain APIs. Conversely, your agents, when calling external services, might need to present VCs from your DID that the external service recognizes. Establishing this trust may require some governance (e.g., agreements to trust each other’s DID issuances), but it’s largely a configuration/PKI bootstrapping step rather than a need to integrate our IdM systems at runtime. In short, **DIDs and VCs enable *ad hoc federation*** – trust can be established via common standards rather than per-partner custom integration. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

**4. Data Plane Enforcement – Capability-Driven, Identity-Aware:** The enforcement mechanism (the “Tool Gateway” in our design) remains responsible for checking *capabilities* on each action. Under a DID/VC system, the gateway’s logic extends to:

*   **Authenticating the agent’s DID**: e.g., verifying a signature (or DPoP token) to ensure the agent presenting a capability is indeed the subject of that capability. In AIP, a Demonstration of Proof-of-Possession (DPoP) using the agent’s DID keys is mandatory for higher risk tiers – the agent signs the HTTP request (or a challenge) with its private key, and the service uses the DID Document to confirm the public key matches the one in the credential. This prevents stolen tokens from being replayed by impostors.
*   **Validating the credential/VC**: This includes checking the issuer’s signature (which involves resolving the issuer’s DID to get a public key), ensuring the credential hasn’t expired, and checking revocation status via the chosen mechanism (short TTL, registry lookup, etc.). The gateway needs to implement standard VC verification. Many libraries exist for JWT and LD-Proof verification; these should be integrated such that verification is as **deterministic** and fast as possible, using cached trust data when appropriate. (The AIP spec actually provides a detailed Validation Algorithm to ensure any two implementations reach the same result for a given token, emphasizing deterministic, offline verification.) [\[ietf.org\]](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-singla-agent-identity-protocol-01.html)
*   **Evaluating the capability against the request**: This is similar to our existing design – ensure the action and resource being requested are covered by one of the permissions in the credential, and that any constraints (time windows, usage limits, etc.) are satisfied. Because VCs can encode complex constraints (e.g., numerical limits, time windows, IP ranges), the gateway needs a robust interpreter for those. AIP’s approach is to use a standard schema for “capability manifests” and define an **algorithm for computing the effective permissions** by intersecting all applicable constraints from the base grant and any overlay restrictions in delegated tokens. We should adopt a similar approach: systematically intersect constraints from the base capability and each delegation overlay so that the *most restrictive* combination is enforced at each step.
*   **Logging and tying to identity**: Enhance the logging to capture the agent’s DID and the verified chain of delegation. For an internal call, the log entry might record: *Agent `did:example:agent123` called `updateCustomer` API with Credential VC #xyz (issued by `did:example:alice` with roles=“support”)*. For an external call, it might log the partner agent’s DID and the fact it presented a VC from a trusted issuer. These details go into the **Capability Audit Graph**, allowing administrators to answer “which agent (and whose agent) performed this action and under what authority?” with high precision. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

**5. Trust Boundaries and Federation Options:** In a real deployment, one size may not fit all. We suggest planning for **multiple trust tiers**:

*   *Within a single cloud or cluster:* For purely internal traffic (e.g., an agent calling another internal microservice), a full DID/VC ceremony might add unnecessary overhead. You could use a streamlined approach like **SPIFFE** for internal mutual auth between services and agents. SPIFFE provides each workload a short-lived X.509 certificate or JWT (called SVID) that asserts its identity and is automatically trusted within your environment. SPIFFE can federate identities across data centers or clouds without embedding Azures or AWS-specific tokens, i.e., it’s vendor-neutral within the context of cooperating domains. In our architecture, an agent’s internal DID could correspond to its SPIFFE SVID under the hood; the Capability Issuer might even leverage SPIRE (the SPIFFE implementation) to get the agent’s identity and then tie that to the capabilities it issues. This gives us high-performance identity verification inside our domain and a bridge to the DID world when needed (for external interactions). [\[spiffe.io\]](https://spiffe.io/docs/latest/spiffe-about/overview/?ref=blog.identity.foundation)
*   *Across organizations or cloud boundaries:* Use **DIDs + VCs** as the lingua franca. This is analogous to how SAML or OIDC federation works for humans, but more flexible. Instead of pre-negotiated one-off trust agreements, any organization can verify any other’s credentials as long as they trust the DID method or the specific issuer. For example, if our enterprise issues a VC to an agent, any partner that trusts our enterprise’s DID (or the consortium ledger it’s anchored in) will accept the capability. Conversely, our systems can be made to trust VCs from a partner’s DID. In effect, **DIDs/VCs play the role of a decentralized federation protocol**.

**6. Security and Compliance Considerations in a DID-based System:**

Adopting DIDs and VCs aligns well with a Zero Trust approach and can meet high-level security requirements, but it’s important to prepare for certain scenarios:

*   **Bootstrapping trust:** Decide which DID methods and issuers you will trust. For an internal pilot, you might operate your own DID method (e.g., a private DID registry or simply did:web at first). Long-term, consider participating in trust communities or consortiums – for example, cloud providers might agree to trust each other’s DIDs for agent identities, or industry groups might host a shared ledger for member DIDs. The IETF AIP draft registers a new method `did:aip` specifically for agent identities, with the idea that each environment could run an **AIP Registry** accessible via the principal’s DID Document. Determining the governance of such a registry (public blockchain vs consortium vs private service) is a strategic decision: public networks maximize vendor-independence and censorship-resistance, while private/consortium ledgers or DNS-based methods offer better performance and control for enterprise use.
*   **Performance optimizations:** As noted, verifying a DID-authored credential involves multiple cryptographic steps (DID resolution, signature checks, possibly fetching revocation info). To keep agent interactions fast, implement caching at every level: cache resolved DID Documents (with appropriate expiration based on their metadata), cache recently seen valid tokens (to skip re-verification if the token ID has been seen and is still valid), and use local mirrors for any blockchain lookups if using ledger-based DIDs. Also consider the impact on your throughput: measure and tune the crypto libraries (for instance, Ed25519 and secp256r1 signature verification are quite fast – sub-millisecond – whereas JSON-LD Canonicalization for LD-Proofs can be heavier). You may choose JWT-based VCs for better performance in early stages, transitioning to LD-Proofs if needed for features like selective disclosure or compatibility with certain ecosystems.
*   **Revocation and monitoring:** As detailed earlier, multiple strategies (short TTLs, status lists, push notifications) should be combined. An **incident response playbook** must cover scenarios like a DID key compromise or malicious agent: in a DID system, “revocation” might mean publishing a revocation record to a ledger or registry and propagating that to all verification points. Expect a time window (maybe a few minutes) during which a revoked credential might still be accepted, unless you have push-based notification. Plan compensating controls for that gap (e.g., anomaly detection to spot suspicious bursts of activity and responsive network-level blocking as a backstop). In practice, this is not very different from how one handles a compromised API key or OAuth token, except that the revocation must be communicated in a decentralized way. AIP’s approach, again, is instructive: Tier 2 (which covers “sensitive operations, default mode”) mandates *real-time revocation checking on every request* against the issuer’s registry service. Tier 1 (less sensitive) allows leaner checking (e.g., batched CRLs). We can adopt similar tiered revocation: for high-criticality agent actions (e.g., financial transactions), require online status verification or even pre-action confirmation from a control plane; for low-risk actions (e.g., read-only info retrieval), allow cached verification with frequent refresh.
*   **Compliance & audit:** On one hand, the use of decentralized identity can simplify compliance by providing clear evidence of consent and delegation. For instance, if regulators ask “Which human authorized the actions of this AI agent?”, you could produce the chain of verifiable credentials linking the agent back to a human principal’s approval. This is far stronger evidence than relying on internal logs or hoping the agent preserved the user’s instructions. On the other hand, new privacy considerations arise: an agent’s DID could be considered personal data (if it can be linked to a user). Fortunately, DID/VC technology supports privacy-preserving techniques (e.g., *pairwise DIDs* that prevent correlation, and selective disclosure where the agent only shows the necessary part of its credentials). You can design the system so that when an agent interacts outside, it uses a special pairwise DID per partner service, limiting correlation, and discloses only the fact that it has a specific capability without revealing extraneous info. This is done via zero-knowledge proof techniques or VC selective disclosure and is already a known benefit of DIDs/VCs in identity systems. For example, an agent could prove “I have a credential issued by Enterprise X allowing me to make purchases up to $100” without revealing its agent ID or the full credential content, if the VC is designed for ZK proofs. These features can help comply with data minimization principles. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/) [\[community.cisco.com\]](https://community.cisco.com/t5/security-blogs/a-new-identity-framework-for-ai-agents/ba-p/5294337)

***

## Recommended Hybrid Architecture: DID-Enabled Capability Governance

Bringing it all together, below is a high-level view of the proposed hybrid architecture for an agent identity and authorization system that is both cloud-agnostic and multi-organization friendly:

*   **Enterprise DID and Trust Root:** Your organization establishes a root of trust in the form of a DID. For example, `did:web:enterprise.com` could serve as the “root principal” identifier for your company, or you might use an existing public decentralized DID method. This DID represents the authority of your IAM system. Its corresponding DID Document contains (a) the public keys used to sign credentials (similar to an OAuth JWKS), and (b) a service endpoint for a **Capability/Registry Service** that helps with agent on- and off-boarding. External verifiers resolve this DID to fetch your keys and verify credentials you’ve issued. Internally, this enterprise DID can be mapped to your tenant in Azure AD or any other IdP – it’s essentially a globally unique name for “Company X’s identity authority”.

*   **Identity Binding at Agent Creation:** When an agent is created (e.g., a user spawns a new agent instance), the following happens:
    1.  **Agent DID generation:** The agent, or the system on its behalf, generates a new cryptographic key pair and registers a DID. If using did:web, this could mean creating a DID Document on a secure internal service or via an API call to a corporate DID directory. If using did:ion or another on-chain method, the registration might be batched or done asynchronously (to avoid startup latency). Some systems (like Ion) allow DIDs to be created off-chain and **anchored later**; the DID can be used immediately with the assumption it will be anchored within a grace period.
    2.  **Enterprise Authentication:** The user or service that wants the agent launched authenticates to the enterprise IdP (e.g., via OAuth/OIDC login or API key). This provides the link to a human or service principal in the enterprise.
    3.  **Capability Issuance (Principal -> Agent):** The enterprise’s Capability Issuer service (augmented to function as a **VC issuer**) creates a **Capability Credential** for the new agent. This credential includes:
        *   The agent’s DID as the subject.
        *   The issuer field set to the enterprise’s DID (or the user’s own DID, with the enterprise attesting to it).
        *   The list of authorized capabilities (actions/resources), possibly as a JSON schema or list of scopes, including any constraints (expiry, use limits, etc.).
        *   Optionally, a reference to a more detailed **Capability Manifest** stored in the enterprise registry (to allow updates or elaborate policies).
        *   Delegation information: e.g., an attestation that *“Agent DID X is acting on behalf of Human DID Y (who is <User Name> in our directory) with these permissions”*. In AIP, this would be represented by issuing both a **Principal Token** (mapping Y’s authority to X) and a **Capability Manifest** for X. In a VC approach, it could be a single credential containing both the delegation (Y → X) and the permissions granted, or two linked credentials. The exact packaging can vary, but the system will treat them as the agent’s “passport” to be presented for access.
        *   The credential is then signed using the enterprise’s DID key.
    4.  **Agent Key Provenance:** The issuance process should also include a step to confirm the agent’s public key. In practice, this could be done by including the agent’s DID (which contains the public key) in the credential and having the agent prove control of the private key (e.g., by signing a challenge from the issuer). This prevents malicious actors from getting capabilities bound to someone else’s DID. It’s analogous to OIDC’s requirement for a *redirect URI* and token binding, but here it’s done with signatures at the time of issuance.
    5.  **Registry Update:** The enterprise’s Capability Registry is updated with a record of the new agent, its issued capabilities, and any delegation constraints. This is used for internal governance (e.g., to display what access each agent has, possibly in an admin UI) and for supporting revocation. Depending on design, this registry might also publish a *transparency log entry* or CRL that external parties can consult to ensure the credential is still valid. (In AIP, the registry plays a crucial role in both bootstrapping – agents register their keys & manifests with it – and revocation tracking. In our architecture, the registry function might be split across an internal database and an external transparency mechanism, but the concept is similar.)

*   **Data Plane Authentication & Authorization:** Whenever the agent uses a tool or accesses a service:
    1.  **Authentication** – The agent proves its identity by presenting its DID and a proof of key possession. This could be as simple as using its DID key to sign the request (the service has to check the signature against the DID Document, which might be cached). Alternatively, the agent’s capability credential could itself serve as an auth token (if it’s a JWT, it might be presented in an `Authorization` header). In AIP, the “Credential Token” is a self-contained JWT that the agent presents to the Relying Party for both identity and authZ, alongside a DPoP-style proof that the agent is the holder of the key. Regardless of method, the service ends up with cryptographic assurance of the agent’s identity (DID) and the attached permissions.
    2.  **Authorization** – The Tool Gateway (or service’s policy enforcement point) verifies the presented capability against the requested operation:
        *   Checks the signatures and validity of all credentials in the chain (e.g., using the issuers’ DIDs to get keys). The chain typically starts with a human/organizational DID that the service knows or is configured to trust. For instance, if `did:enterprise:xyz` is one of your trusted partners, the service will accept capabilities ultimately bearing that DID’s signature. (In practice, there could be a registry of trusted issuers or a rule like “any DID that has a DNS name matching my partner’s domain and a valid certificate” if using did:web).
        *   Extracts the effective set of permissions after applying all constraints from delegation overlays. For example, the enterprise might have granted “read/write to database X”, but a user overlay has restricted it to “read-only”, and a parent agent further overlayed “only rows in Department=42”. The service computes the intersection: effectively, “read-only on DB X limited to Dept=42”, and then ensures the agent’s request falls within that.
        *   Enforces **proof of possession**: ensure the request is tied to the agent’s key. The gateway might require a cryptographic nonce or use a protocol like DPoP or mTLS such that the agent cannot relay its token to another party without detection.
        *   If everything checks out, the action is allowed; otherwise it’s denied. **No additional identity lookup is needed** – the combination of DID resolution + credential verification *is* the auth check.
    3.  **Service-Specific Policies:** The service can still apply its own local authorization logic *on top of* the capability check if needed. For instance, if the service has an internal policy that certain operations require payment or a further human verification, it can enforce that. The capability tells you what the agent *could* do; the service is free to impose stricter rules. The key is that the capability is a ceiling on authority – the agent cannot exceed it no matter what – and it’s a portable contract of what’s allowed, but it doesn’t prevent more stringent checks.

*   **Monitoring & Response:** All actions (allowed or denied) are logged with the agent’s DID and credential ID. In effect, your **audit trail becomes a set of verifiable statements**. If an incident occurs (say a rogue agent transferred funds improperly), you can use the logged credential ID to retrieve the signed delegation chain and determine:
    *   Which principal ultimately approved that action (and that principal’s identity might be a DID corresponding to a human user or a client system). [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
    *   Whether the agent exceeded its given authority or simply abused an allowed authority.
    *   Whether the credential was supposed to be expired or revoked at the time (indicating a potential enforcement failure if it was used after revocation).
        This forensic rigor is a major improvement over traditional logs. It’s akin to having each access token act as a signed power-of-attorney letter that is archived for later inspection. Furthermore, because the system is cryptographically verifiable, the audit records could be shared with external auditors or regulators to prove agent actions and delegations in a zero-trust fashion (they can verify the signatures themselves). [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Incident response (Kill-switch):** In the hybrid model, you have two means to halt a rogue agent:
    1.  **Centralized kill** – Since you control the internal environment, you can always revoke the agent’s credentials and kill its process. For internal incidents, your existing kill-switch (as designed in the earlier phases) remains effective. The agent’s runtime can be signaled to stop or its container isolated.
    2.  **Distributed kill** – In addition, propagate revocation of the agent’s DID or credentials to prevent any *external* parties from accepting its tokens. For example, you could publish a signed *revocation attestation* to a known location (your DID’s registry or a public revocation list) that external services check. AIP’s approach is to have a mandatory check against the issuer’s CRL for certain tiers of tokens, which is refreshed at least every 15 minutes. We might implement a similar approach: e.g., require that our agents’ credentials include a URL for a JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) or status list, and update that list immediately upon revocation. External services that cache our JWKS would naturally fail to validate a token if we’ve removed the corresponding key or flagged it (this is analogous to OAuth token revocation by key rotation).
        *Implementation tip:* An emerging pattern is to couple short-lived credentials with a **continuous assurance channel**. The credential proves the agent had authority as of issuance; a parallel mechanism (like a secure publish-subscribe on status updates, or periodic challenges) provides ongoing assurance that the agent is still good. This is advanced, but consider it for high-risk interactions. For example, a long-running transaction might require the agent to re-confirm its status via a quick challenge-response every minute. This conceit is present in AIP’s design via “Tier 2 real-time revocation checking” and an optional Real-time Publication Notification Protocol (RPNP) for pushing revocation events to relying parties within seconds.

### Architectural Diagram of the Hybrid Model (Referential)

To illustrate, here’s a conceptual flow for an agent from creation to action in a hybrid DID+capability system:

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

[\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

**Performance considerations:** A well-designed hybrid system can achieve low-latency authorization decisions. Cryptographic verification (signatures, DID resolution) can often be cached or accelerated with hardware. Google, Microsoft, and others are actively working on these standards because they foresee the need to handle “billions of autonomous agents” with acceptable overhead. Our own design can leverage this work (e.g., using proven JWT libraries for VC-JWTs, and employing highly optimized DID resolvers and JSON-LD processors for VC-LD). We should also partition the problem space by using **contextual cues**: for instance, an internal microservice may only accept credentials from our enterprise DID (making verification a simple local key check), whereas a public-facing API might accept a range of issuer DIDs and thus incur extra resolution steps. By using tiers and context, we ensure that decentralization overhead is incurred only when necessary (for open ecosystem interactions) and not when simpler assumptions hold (within a closed environment or pre-federated partner context). [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

## When to Use Centralized IAM vs. DIDs vs. Hybrid

Choosing the right approach depends on your collaboration model, risk tolerance, and infrastructure maturity. Here are some guidelines:

*   **Use existing centralized IAM + internal capabilities** when all agent interactions occur *within a single trust domain* (your cloud or on-prem environment), and you control both the agents and services. Traditional IdP-issued tokens combined with an internal capability enforcement layer can be sufficient here, and they offer a simpler implementation. This scenario is common in initial deployments or closed-loop systems (e.g., an agent automating tasks entirely within one company’s cloud). You’ll benefit from faster setup using familiar OAuth/OIDC patterns for initial user<->agent delegation, and you avoid the added complexity of DID/VC infrastructure. *However*, keep in mind this limits the agents’ world: if tomorrow you need them to interact with a partner’s APIs or user agents from outside your org, you’ll hit the limitations of this model. Also, ensure your internal identities are not over-scoped: even within one domain, avoid giving agents blanket user tokens; instead issue them reduced-scope capabilities (via mechanisms like OAuth 2.0 “delegation” grants or impersonation with constraints).

*   **Use a fully decentralized DID+VC model** when agents operate in an **open ecosystem or across organizational boundaries by default**. For example, autonomous agents in a supply chain network with participants from multiple companies, or consumer-facing agents that interact with third-party services on behalf of users, would benefit from a self-sovereign identity approach. DIDs shine in scenarios where no single authority is “in charge” of identity for all parties. If your agents need to be recognized by external services without each service manually whitelisting your specific IdP, giving them DID-based credentials is an elegant solution. A pure DID/VC approach also aligns with emerging **self-sovereign identity (SSI)** principles and may future-proof your architecture as standards evolve. That said, a pure decentralized model may be overkill if you don’t actually require that level of global interoperability. It also demands robust internal expertise in cryptographic key management and emerging protocols – a high upfront cost. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Use a hybrid approach** in most realistic enterprise scenarios – particularly during the transition period to more decentralized infrastructure, or when you have a mix of closed and open environments. In a hybrid model:
    *   Internally, agents authenticate and get capabilities through your existing IAM (leveraging its security features and user consent flows). Nothing changes for internal services: they see the same familiar tokens or SAML assertions, except those tokens carry (or reference) capability restrictions. You enforce them with a policy engine as we’ve designed.
    *   Externally, when agents roam or interact across org boundaries, they switch to DID-based identities and present verifiable capability credentials that outsiders can validate. Here you achieve the goal of **no “phoning home” to your IdP** – external verifiers need not integrate with your IAM, they only need to understand standard DID/VC processes. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)
    *   A practical example: Suppose your internal agent, with AD-backed identity, needs to call a partner’s API. With hybrid identity, your agent’s call is authorized by a VC signed with your enterprise DID. The partner’s service resolves that DID (using DNS or blockchain), gets your public key and perhaps checks your company’s reputation or a contract ID in the credential, then allows the action. Conversely, if the partner’s agent calls your API, your service will verify the partner’s credential via their DID. No pre-configured client IDs or secret keys exchanged – just mutual recognition of each other’s decentralized identifiers, potentially facilitated by an allowlist of partner DIDs or membership in a common trust network (e.g., both companies being part of a specific consortium or complying with an interoperability standard).

In summary, **use the simplest model that meets your needs, but plan for growth and openness**. If your agent ecosystem is initially small and closed, start with a centralized approach for expedience – but lay groundwork for DIDs by assigning each agent a unique identifier (even if it’s just a UUID or URN for now) and designing your capability format to be easily extended to a VC or JWT form that includes issuer signatures. As your agents begin to cross organizational or cloud boundaries, introduce DIDs and VCs for those interactions – possibly first in a pilot with a friendly partner or within a multi-tenant test environment – so you can iron out the kinks (e.g., how to manage DID rotations, how to integrate with your existing revocation processes). Over time, you can increase the use of decentralized identity internally as well, which might reduce friction in M\&A situations, cloud migrations, or regulatory audits.

**When to choose what:**

*   **Centralized IAM + Capabilities:** Best for contained environments and rapid initial deployment. Leverages familiar tools (OAuth/OIDC) and keeps latency to a minimum. Be cautious: this mode has limited ability to differentiate or constrain individual agents acting under one identity, and it requires strong perimeter controls since agents are essentially “logged in” as full users. Use this when speed is critical and all participants are under one authority, but recognize it won’t scale to broader ecosystems. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Fully Decentralized (DID + VC + Capabilities):** Ideal for open ecosystems, marketplaces, or consortiums where agents from many origins interact. Also necessary when **user-controlled agents** (outside enterprise control) need to access services – for instance, a future where users run personal AIs that interact with your business. DIDs/VCs enable you to verify a *user’s* consent or an *issuer’s* endorsement on an incoming request from an unknown agent. Use this when you need **zero-trust posture by design**, maximum interoperability, and cryptographic auditability across org boundaries – and when your team is prepared to manage the complexity (or when products/services are available to manage it for you). [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

*   **Hybrid:** Likely the scenario for most enterprises in the near term. Use decentralized identity for what it’s best at – cross-domain trust and fine-grained delegation – while using centralized IAM for what it’s best at – integrating with legacy apps and human user workflows. In concrete terms, continue to let users authenticate via SSO to your portal to launch agents (that’s a usability requirement), but in the background, translate those sessions into DID-based credentials for the agents. Continue to use your existing internal SAML/OIDC tokens *within a single cloud or VPC*, but require DID+VCs at the microservice boundaries when crossing out of the protected network (much like one would place an API gateway for external traffic). Over time, as decentralized tech matures, you can shrink the “centralized zone” and move more trust to the decentralized layer. This path is echoed by experts: *“start with agents having decentralised IDs for outside interactions, while keeping your internal OAuth setup for now”*. It offers a smooth upgrade path with minimal disruption to existing systems. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/)

In conclusion, **distributed identity (DID/VC) is a powerful complement to capability-based security**, enabling a truly vendor-agnostic, scalable trust model for autonomous agents. It provides the missing piece of the puzzle: a way to know *which agent* (and ultimately whose agent) is acting, even in a sea of machine identities, while still not having to *trust* that agent’s own word about who it is. Instead, trust is anchored in cryptographic proofs and endorsements from known authorities. By carefully blending this with your current IAM and capability framework, you can achieve a system that is **secure by design, highly interoperable, and prepared for the future** of multi-cloud and multi-organization AI agent ecosystems. This hybrid approach is aligned with the latest standards efforts (IETF, W3C, DIF) and best practices for zero-trust in AI systems, setting the stage for a robust, scalable governance of agent identity and access in the coming years. [\[blog.ident...foundation\]](https://blog.identity.foundation/building-ai-trust-at-scale-4/), [\[community.cisco.com\]](https://community.cisco.com/t5/security-blogs/a-new-identity-framework-for-ai-agents/ba-p/5294337)
